title: Ceropegia pilosa
intro: Ceropegia pilosa, also known as the hairy rosette or rope vine, is a plant species in the Apocynaceae family characterized by its unique, rope-like stems and hairy leaves. Traditionally, Ceropegia pilosa has been used in folk medicine to treat various health issues, including inflammation, fever, and digestive problems, due to its potential health benefits such as antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and antioxidant properties. The bioactive constituents of Ceropegia pilosa include alkaloids, glycosides, and saponins, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, Ceropegia pilosa is often prepared as a decoction, infusion, or tincture, and is commonly used to treat fever, cough, and respiratory issues.
taxonomy: Ceropegia pilosa is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Apocynaceae. This family is commonly known as the dogbane or oleander family. Within the Apocynaceae family, Ceropegia pilosa is further classified as part of the subfamily Asclepiadoideae. The genus Ceropegia is a group of plants commonly known as the string of hearts or Rosary vine. Ceropegia pilosa is native to tropical regions, where it grows in woodland and rocky areas.
